title: How would you structure a database schema to support document ingestion for a RAG pipeline?
prompt:
How would you structure a database schema to support document ingestion for a RAG pipeline?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
Separate parent document metadata from chunk embeddings to support atomic invalidation, deduplication, and ACL filters. A `documents` table holds `id`, `file_hash`, `status`, and `version`; a separate `document_chunks` table holds `id`, a `document_id` foreign key back to `documents`, `chunk_index`, `embedding_model`, `embedding_version`, and `vector_store_id`. Why this schema works: cascade invalidation — linking chunks to `document_id` lets you supersede all of a document's chunks atomically the moment the source document updates; embedding model tracking — recording `embedding_model` allows incremental migrations when you upgrade to a new embedding model or dimension size; ACL governance — join against a separate `document_permissions` table at retrieval time to enforce per-user read access on top of the vector search.

**Takeaway:** Never store chunk embeddings as the only source of truth — a parent `documents` table with a foreign-keyed `document_chunks` table gives you atomic document-level updates, embedding-model migration tracking, and ACL enforcement, none of which a flat chunk table can support cleanly.
hint_notes: Two tables: documents (id, file_hash, status, version) and document_chunks (fk to documents, embedding_model/version, vector_store_id) — enables atomic invalidation, embedding migrations, and ACL joins.
